quotes.text: Let none out of anger of resentment wish harm to another.
Let us then live happily, not hating those who hate us. In the midst of those who hate us, let us dwell free from hatred.
For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time; hatred ceases by love; this is ans old rule.
To the self-reliant there is strength and joy.
Let him not grieve for that which is lost.
Not from weeping or grieving will any obtain peace of mind.
At first my sorrowing heart was heavy; but now my sorrow has brought forth only profit.
Give to him that asketh, even thought it be but a little.
Yours guileless heart loves to exercise its charity.
Those in whom there is neither deceit nor arrogance, who are free from cupidity, selfishness, desire, upon such in due time should people bestow their offerings.
He delights in giving so far as he is able.
quotes.source: Questions of king Milinda (Book 4, ch. 1, sec. 9)

quotes.text: Earnestly practise every good work.
Not to be weary in well-doing.
A Hero in beneficence.
Pure in morals, and assiduous in doing good.
Make no idols of any kind.
When pure rules of conduct are observed, then there is true religion.
Wherein does religion consist? In (committing) the least possible harm, in (doing) abundance of good, in (the practice of) pity, love, truth, and likewise purity of life.

(Not superstitious rites, but) kindness to slaves and servants, reverence towards venerable persons, self-control with respect to living creatures, … these and similar (virtuous actions are the rites which ought indeed to be performed).
The practice of religion involves as a first principle a loving, compassionate heart for all creatures.
Shall we in worshipping slay that which hath life? this is like those who practise wisdom, and the way of religious abstraction, but neglect the rules of moral conduct.
How can a system requiring the infliction of misery on other beings be called a religious system? … To seek a good by doing an evil is surely no safe plan.
Unto the dumb lips of his flock he lent sad pleading words, showing how man, who prays for mercy to the gods, in merciless.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 5)

quotes.text: I Then will ask you, If a man, in worshipping, … sacrifices a sheep, and so does well, wherefore not his child, … and so do better? Surely … there is no merit in killing a sheep!
Nor [shall one] lay upon the brow of innocent bound beasts one hair’s weight of that answer all must give for all things done amiss or wrongfully.
quotes.source: Sir Edwin Arnold (Light of Asia, bk. 5)

quotes.text: Doing no injure to anyone, dwell in the world full of love and kindness.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 3, sec. 35)

quotes.text: Ministering to the worthy, doing harm to none, Always ready to render reverence to whom it is due, Loving righteousness and righteous conversation, Ever willing to hear what may profit another.
Scrupulously avoiding all wicked actions Reverently performing all virtuous ones; Purifying his intention from all selfish ends; This is the doctrine of all the Buddhas.
Instruct yourself (more and more) in the highest morality.
They must cultivate a feeling of deep shame for their sin.
May my thoughts, now small and narrow, expand in the next existence, that I may understand the precepts… thoroughly, and never break them or be guilty of trespasses.
quotes.source: Inscription in Temple of Nakhon Vat

quotes.text: Religion he looks upon as his best ornament.
The sinner is never beautiful.
Use no perfume but sweetness of thoughts.
Wealth and beauty, scented flowers and ornaments like these, are not to be compared for grace with moral rectitude!
Beautiful is she beyond conception, having the beauty of the noblest of womankind.
Look not upon a woman unchastely.
